celebration: Leaf #5 — the ng hum. Two letters that make one nasal hum at the back of the mouth. Half the Pattern Tree's first branch is now in leaf.

songs.title: Ng-Ng-King!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
Ng! Ng! Ng-ng-ng!
(nnng… nnng… nnng…)

[Hook]
Ng at the END — hummm!
Two letters, ONE hum!
King! King! Sing! Sing!
Feel it in your nose! (nnng!)

[Verse 2]
Ng! Ng! Ng-ng-ng!
It hides at the end of the word!
Ring! Ring! Long! Long!
Hum it at the back! (nnng!)

[Verse 3]
Ng! Ng! Ng-ng-ng!
The king can sing a song!
Song! Song! Wing! Wing!
Hum it long and strong! (nnng!)

[Verse 4]
Ng! Ng! Ng-ng-ng!
Bang the gong — bang, BANG!
Hang! Hung! Bang! Gong!
Feel it in your nose! (nnng!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! No hum! Not NG!
(nnng… nnng… nnng…)

[Outro]
King! King! Sing! Sing!
The king can sing! (nnng!)
Ng! Ng! Ng-ng-ng!
(hummm… quiet now…)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: ng is a single nasal /ŋ/ — a HUM held at the back of the mouth, felt in the nose — never the letter names 'en-gee' and never split into /n/ + /g/. It only ever ENDS words or syllables, never starts one; the song puts it at the end every time. EAL note: Mandarin HAS a final -ng but reduces or drops it, so children clip 'sing' to 'sin' — the sustained '(nnng…)' hum with a hand on the nose IS the corrective drill. Never sing letter names.

songs.title: The King Can Sing
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
The king can sing!
The king can sing!
Sing it long and strong!
The king can sing!

[Verse 2]
A long, long song!
The king can sing it long!
Bang the gong! (bang! bang!)
The king can sing!

[Verse 3]
Chick can sing along!
Sheep can hum the song!
Sing it! Sing it! (nnng!)
The king can sing!

[Outro]
The song… so long… (hush…)
A potato?! (POTATO!)
The king can sing at a potato?! (sing, king, sing!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'The king can sing.' Verse 2 lands 'long', 'song' and 'gong'; verse 3 brings Chick and Sheep in on the chorus. Hold the sung /ŋ/ hum on every ng word. Whisper the outro, potato hits loud.

spreads.artNote: A small round friendly king in a golden crown, chest puffed, mouth just opening. Warm and comic, not grand or scary.
The king mid-note, one hand on his chest, a single music curl rising. Delight.
The king belting it out, crown tipping, a big swirl of music notes filling the spotlight. Full volume.
A ribbon of music notes stretching right off both edges of the spread — the visual joke of a very long song. The king sings on, eyes closed.
Chick beside the king, beak wide, tiny music note above her. Joining in with total commitment.
Sheep humming along, eyes closed, a low woolly note curling up. The trio in harmony.
A big brass gong on a stand, Chick swinging a mallet, a burst of ripple-rings and BANG lines. The song gets a beat.
Curtain-call pose: king, Chick and Sheep together mid-bow, notes settling around them. A triumphant little concert.
A single potato sits on a velvet cushion in the spotlight and the king, arms wide, is serenading it earnestly. Chick and Sheep exchange a look. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it.

extras: PATTERN TREE: leaf #5. Read sing/ring/king/long aloud and hang the ng leaf.

THE HUM: ng is a single sound made at the back of the mouth — put a hand on the nose and feel it buzz. It only ever ENDS a word or syllable; it never starts one. Contrast with a quick /n/ then a quick /g/ to prove it is neither.

EAL EXPLICIT DRILL (this week's whole point): Mandarin drops or clips the final -ng, so 'sing' becomes 'sin', 'king' becomes 'kin'. Minimal-pair the class: sing/sin, ring/rin, king/kin, thing/thin, song/son. Hold the hum long on the -ng member every time.
Moveable alphabet: build ng welded onto the end — si-ng, ri-ng, ki-ng, lo-ng.

teacherFocus: ng is a single nasal hum /ŋ/ made at the back of the mouth — put a hand on the nose to feel it. Teach two facts: it makes ONE sound (not /n/+/g/), and it only ever ENDS a word or syllable, never begins one. This is an explicit EAL week: Mandarin has a final -ng but reduces it, so children clip 'sing' to 'sin' and 'king' to 'kin'. Drill the minimal pairs hard — sing/sin, ring/rin, king/kin, thing/thin — holding the sung hum long on the -ng member. Hang leaf #5; the first branch of the Pattern Tree is now half in leaf.
